title: Bathroom Tall Boy Cabinet
summary: A tall, narrow two-door bathroom cabinet built as two stacked plywood carcasses — a base and a top box — with all pocket holes hidden top and bottom before they're joined.
description:
Building this cabinet as two separate carcasses — a base box and a top box — rather than one tall case is what lets every pocket hole disappear: the top carcass gets its pocket holes on its own top edge, and the base carcass gets its on the underside, so once the two are stacked and joined, nothing shows on the visible faces. Rip your plywood into 15-1/4" strips first, since every carcass part comes from those strips.

Each carcass gets an extra bay support at the back for structure, pocket holes facing upward into the ends — the base carcass gets a second bay support as well, with its pocket holes facing the back of the cabinet, since **the base carcass also carries a fixed bottom shelf that needs planned-ahead pocket holes into its underside**. Get this planning right before you build the bottom shelf in, since drilling into an assembled carcass afterward is much harder than planning it up front.

The 1/4" plywood back panel is glued and nailed on last, working around the carcass to square everything up against the back panel's factory-square edges — **this is what squares the whole cabinet**, not clamps or measuring. Shelves can be fixed in place or hung on shelf pins for adjustability; if going adjustable, trim the shelf length about 1/8" shorter so it can be maneuvered into place without binding.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/4" strips, full sheet
1/4" underlayment or hardwood plywood, full sheet
Frameless full overlay concealed hinges, pair

cutList.material: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/4" strips, full sheet
cutList.note: Cut first from the ripped strips.

cutList.material: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/4" strips, full sheet
1/4" underlayment or hardwood plywood, full sheet

steps.title: Rip the strips and cut the panels
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood sheet into 15-1/4"-wide strips first — every carcass part comes off these, so an inconsistent rip width shows up as a cabinet that will not close square. Cut the four carcass sides at 33-1/2" first, then the five 28-1/2" pieces that become the two carcass tops, the two bottoms and the fixed shelf. The material is exactly sufficient and no more: three strips take 33-1/2" plus 33-1/2" plus 28-1/2" on two of them and three 28-1/2" pieces on the third, which leaves about a quarter inch of slack in the worst strip, so measure twice and keep your kerfs tight. Cut the five bay supports at 28-1/2" from the 1x3s, three from one board and two from the other, and leave the 1/4" back panel oversized for now — you will trim it to the assembled cabinet rather than to a number.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/4" strips, full sheet
1/4" underlayment or hardwood plywood, full sheet

steps.title: Build the two carcasses
steps.body: Build two identical boxes, a base carcass and a top carcass, each from two 33-1/2" sides and two 28-1/2" panels. The 28-1/2" panels fit between the sides, which run the full 33-1/2" height — 28-1/2" plus 3/4" of side at each end is exactly the 30" width the back panel is cut to, and that is what fixes the assembly this way round. Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the ends of the 28-1/2" panels and drive 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws. Put the top carcass's pocket holes on its top edge and the base carcass's on its bottom edge, so that once the two boxes are stacked and joined every hole is either against the floor or above eye level and none of them shows on a visible face. Clamp each joint flush before driving; plywood end grain lets a screw pull a panel proud.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Add the bay supports
steps.body: Fit a 28-1/2" 1x3 bay support across the back of each carcass, pocket holes drilled to face upward into the side panels, and fasten with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws — this is what keeps a 30"-wide plywood box from racking before the back goes on. The base carcass gets a second support, this one with its pocket holes facing the back of the cabinet. Plan and drill that second one before the shelf goes anywhere near it: it has to receive pocket holes from the underside of the fixed bottom shelf, and drilling into a support already buried inside an assembled carcass is much harder than doing it on the bench.

steps.title: Add the bottom shelf
steps.body: Fit the remaining 28-1/2" panel into the base carcass as the fixed bottom shelf, fastening it with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws driven up into its underside so no hole shows from above. Mark its height on both sides of the carcass from the same reference edge and clamp a straight offcut across as a ledger to hold it level while you drive. Check the shelf for level in both directions before the last screw goes in — a fixed shelf is not adjustable later.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Attach the back panel
steps.body: Stack the two carcasses, join them, and then cut the 1/4" back panel to the assembled cabinet rather than to a stated size — two 33-1/2" carcasses stack to 67", not the 68" the cut list gives, so measure the real stack. Glue the back edges of both carcasses, lay a factory edge of the panel against one side of the cabinet and a factory corner at the top, and nail down with 3/4" brad nails working out from that corner, pulling each side and top into line with the panel's edge as you go. This is what squares the whole cabinet — a factory-cut 1/4" panel is a truer reference than anything you can measure across a 5-1/2 ft case, so do not clamp the carcass square first and hope the back agrees.
materials: 1/4" underlayment or hardwood plywood, full sheet

steps.title: Install the shelves
steps.body: Fit the remaining shelves either fixed in place, pocket-screwed like the bottom shelf, or hung on pins for adjustability. If you go adjustable, trim about 1/8" off the shelf length first — a shelf cut to the exact opening cannot be angled into a closed carcass without scraping both side panels. Drill the pin holes with a depth stop and index every hole off the same bottom edge so the pairs on the two sides land at identical heights; a shelf that rocks means one hole is off.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Build and hang the doors
steps.body: Iron or apply the 3/4" edge banding to the door edges and trim it flush before hanging anything. The two 14-3/4" doors are meant to sit side by side over one carcass: together they span 29-1/2" of the cabinet's 30" width, leaving about 1/4" of reveal at each outer edge for a full overlay. Hold both doors up against the actual carcass before you commit to hinge positions — the doors are 30-3/4" tall against a 33-1/2" carcass, so where that extra 2-3/4" falls is a decision you make on the cabinet, not from the cut list. Mount the frameless full-overlay hinges, then use their built-in adjustment to bring the gap between the two doors even top to bottom.
materials: Frameless full overlay concealed hinges, pair

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces and apply cabinet paint.
